pages.text: Estradiol is a steroid, and so it is a somewhat complex molecule. As such, it would be extremely difficult to synthesize from scratch, as well as ensure only the correct enantiomer is created.
It is for these reasons that we choose a $(o)starting material$() that has a similar chemical structure, and then modify it to create the desired product.

In the human and animal body, steroidal hormones are created from $(o)sterols$(), which are abundant in our foods, let it be cholesterol from meat or eggs, or phytosterols from plant foods. Sterols thus seem like a practical choice for the synthesis of estradiol.

Sterols can be extracted from soybeans by maceration in 64% ethanol: place soybeans in a cauldron full of 64% ethanol, bring it to a boil, and wait. The desired sterols will be dissolved in the ethanol and then crash out as crystals.
